geographiclib 0.4.2.9002
  • Reference
  • Articles
  • Changelog

Three numbers, one polygon

Source: vignettes/articles/measurement.Rmd
measurement.Rmd

Here is a small polygon in EPSG:2154 (RGF93 / Lambert-93, France). We want its perimeter and area. How many different answers can we get?

options(digits = 9)
sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)
## Spherical geometry (s2) switched off
xy <- cbind(
  x = c(320102.67, 320111.85, 320131.82, 320134.33, 320138.50,
        320141.93, 320135.33, 320118.58, 320113.45, 320105.18, 320102.67),
  y = c(6845954.56, 6845962.15, 6845971.55, 6845971.90, 6845953.21,
        6845936.12, 6845935.07, 6845931.54, 6845929.91, 6845950.57, 6845954.56)
)
p   <- sf::st_sf(geometry = sf::st_sfc(sf::st_polygon(list(xy)), crs = "EPSG:2154"))
pna <- p; sf::st_crs(pna) <- NA
pll <- sf::st_transform(p, "EPSG:4326")
ll  <- terra::project(xy, from = terra::crs(terra::vect(p)), to = "EPSG:4326")

Four scenarios: projected CRS, NA CRS, longlat with s2 off, longlat with s2 on. geographiclib is the independent Karney reference; it operates on longlat coordinates so the projected and NA CRS rows are not applicable.

Scenario 1: projected CRS (EPSG:2154) 

## perimeter
as.numeric(sf::st_perimeter(p))
#> [1] 129.248935
as.numeric(sf::st_length(sf::st_boundary(p)))
#> [1] 129.248935
terra::perim(terra::vect(p))
#> [1] 129.248935

## area
as.numeric(sf::st_area(p))
#> [1] 1049.6505
terra::expanse(terra::vect(p), unit = "m")
#> [1] 1050.2426

## geographiclib reference
geographiclib::polygon_area(ll)
#> $area
#> [1] -1050.2426
#> 
#> $perimeter
#> [1] 129.285384
#> 
#> $n
#> [1] 11

sf and terra agree on perimeter (planar). For area, terra reprojects internally and returns the ellipsoidal answer; sf returns the planar shoelace result. geographiclib confirms the ellipsoidal value.

Scenario 2: NA CRS 

## perimeter
as.numeric(sf::st_perimeter(pna))
#> [1] 129.248935
terra::perim(terra::vect(pna))
#> Warning: [perim] unknown CRS. Results can be wrong
#> [1] 129.248935

## area
as.numeric(sf::st_area(pna))
#> [1] 1049.6505
terra::expanse(terra::vect(pna), unit = "m")
#> Warning: [expanse] unknown CRS. Results can be wrong
#> [1] 1049.65051

Both return planar results. terra warns (“unknown CRS, results can be wrong”); sf is silent.

Scenario 3: longlat, s2 off 

sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)

## perimeter
as.numeric(sf::st_perimeter(pll))
#> [1] 129.285384
as.numeric(sf::st_length(sf::st_boundary(pll)))
#> [1] 129.285384
terra::perim(terra::vect(pll))
#> [1] 129.285384

## area
as.numeric(sf::st_area(pll))
#> [1] 1050.2426
terra::expanse(terra::vect(pll), unit = "m")
#> [1] 1050.2426

## geographiclib reference
geographiclib::polygon_area(ll)
#> $area
#> [1] -1050.2426
#> 
#> $perimeter
#> [1] 129.285384
#> 
#> $n
#> [1] 11

All tools agree on the ellipsoidal (Karney) answer.

Scenario 4: longlat, s2 on 

sf::sf_use_s2(TRUE)
#> Spherical geometry (s2) switched on

## perimeter
as.numeric(sf::st_perimeter(pll))
#> [1] 129.09815
as.numeric(sf::st_length(sf::st_boundary(pll)))
#> [1] 129.09815
terra::perim(terra::vect(pll))
#> [1] 129.285384

## area
as.numeric(sf::st_area(pll))
#> [1] 1047.02612
terra::expanse(terra::vect(pll), unit = "m")
#> [1] 1050.2426

## geographiclib reference (unaffected by sf_use_s2)
geographiclib::polygon_area(ll)
#> $area
#> [1] -1050.2426
#> 
#> $perimeter
#> [1] 129.285384
#> 
#> $n
#> [1] 11

sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)  ## restore
#> Spherical geometry (s2) switched off

sf gives the spherical answer (s2 uses radius 6,371,010 m, zero flattening). terra and geographiclib are unaffected by sf_use_s2() and return the ellipsoidal answer throughout.

Summary tables

Perimeter (m)

scenario sf 1.0-21 sf >= 1.0-22 terra geographiclib
projected (EPSG:2154) 129.248935 129.248935 129.248935 † —
NA crs 129.248935 129.248935 129.248935 ‡ —
longlat, s2 off 129.285384 129.285384 129.285384 129.285384
longlat, s2 on ★129.09815 129.285384 129.285384 129.285384

Area (m²)

scenario sf 1.0-21 sf >= 1.0-22 terra geographiclib
projected (EPSG:2154) 1049.651 1049.651 1050.243 † —
NA crs 1049.651 1049.651 1049.651 ‡ —
longlat, s2 off 1050.243 1050.243 1050.243 1050.243
longlat, s2 on 1047.026 1047.026 1050.243 1050.243

† terra::perim and terra::expanse are inconsistent for projected CRS: expanse reprojects internally and returns the ellipsoidal answer; perim trusts the projected coordinates and returns planar. No warning.

‡ terra warns on NA CRS; sf is silent.

★ fixed in sf 1.0-22 (#2541): st_perimeter() now uses ellipsoidal computation when sf_use_s2() is FALSE.

The inconsistencies

1. terra::expanse vs terra::perim (projected CRS) expanse reprojects to longlat internally and applies Karney for any known CRS. perim does not — it returns planar distance for projected inputs and only switches to Karney for longlat. This asymmetry presents a cryptic potential gotch for users; expanse has the more defensible design as terra does generally.

2. sf projected CRS: no ellipsoidal awareness for area sf::st_area() on a projected polygon returns the planar shoelace result with no warning. It does not reproject. The answer is “correct in the map” but not on the Earth. terra::expanse does the extra work silently.

3. sf NA CRS: silent vs terra’s warning Both return planar results with no CRS. terra warns; sf does not.

4. sf < 1.0-22: st_perimeter ignored sf_use_s2() With longlat input and sf_use_s2(FALSE), st_perimeter() still dispatched through s2 giving the spherical answer, while st_length(st_boundary()) correctly gave Karney. Fixed in sf 1.0-22 (#2541).

What to use

For consistent ellipsoidal results across all CRS inputs: terra::expanse() for area, geodist::geodist(..., measure = "geodesic") or geographiclib for perimeter/length. Note that terra::perim() requires longlat input to give the ellipsoidal answer.

If you use sf: transform to longlat and set sf_use_s2(FALSE). Both st_area() and st_length(st_boundary()) then give Karney results. Keep in mind this is a global option — set it deliberately and restore it.

There are other functions in the families of sf and terra for distance calculations (i.e. see package {lwgeom} and {geosphere}) but this article is motivated by the problematic framing users face around expectations and results with very widely used functions.

If you want truly xy-in-map only calculations the only really reliable way is to set crs to NA, this was established by sf early in its history as “good practice” and unfortunately is a clear conclusion from the behaviour we see here. Having functions that do clearly what they seem named to do would be preferable, but as it is users have to navigate these vagaries to be sure of what they are getting. These same principles appy to buffering and nearest-calcuations but we will not explore those implications here besides to point out one example:

Warning message:
In st_voronoi.sfc(st_geometry(x), st_sfc(envelope), dTolerance,  :
  st_voronoi does not correctly triangulate longitude/latitude data

When seeing warnings like this you can wonder “what does correctly” mean here? As we’ve seen there is more than one version of “correct” because that depends on what you intend to do and purpose of your task. A function should simply do what it advertises and (ideally) be consistent within its own context. Declarations of what is correct without significantly more context are specious, at best.

Session info 

sessioninfo::session_info()
#> ─ Session info ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#>  setting  value
#>  version  R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
#>  os       Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS
#>  system   x86_64, linux-gnu
#>  ui       X11
#>  language en
#>  collate  en_AU.UTF-8
#>  ctype    en_AU.UTF-8
#>  tz       Etc/UTC
#>  date     2026-03-05
#>  pandoc   3.4 @ /usr/lib/rstudio-server/bin/quarto/bin/tools/x86_64/ (via rmarkdown)
#> 
#> ─ Packages ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Code from these gists:

  • correct_area.md — area comparison across projections and engines (2023)
  • area_and_crs.md — area with Mercator distortion (2024)
  • real_dist.md — perimeter, three numbers, R-sig-geo May 2025

Developed by Michael Sumner, Charles Karney.
